original_abstract: This research explores the strategies employed by Islamic Religious Education (PAI) teachers at SDN 2 Kesumadadi to cultivate religious character in students through the habituation of reciting the Asma’ al-Husna (the 99 Names of Allah). While the formal curriculum for this level typically covers only four specific names, the PAI teachers introduce the complete set of names to foster deeper spiritual roots and overcome student anxieties regarding Arabic literacy. Using a qualitative field research approach, data were gathered via observation, in-depth interviews, and documentation. The findings reveal that while the collective recitation is integrated into religious lessons rather than a daily morning routine, it serves as a vital pedagogical tool. The integration strategy relies on five key pillars: providing exemplary leadership (uswah hasanah), consistent habituation, proactive mentoring—including the use of Latin transliterations for students struggling with Arabic script—positive reinforcement through appreciation, and the enforcement of discipline. Ultimately, these efforts aim to internalize religious values and enhance the spiritual and emotional intelligence of students within the elementary school environment.
humanized_abstract: Judging from the literature, it appears that many researchers discuss the methods and strategies that are used by teachers to build the religious character of elementary school students. Based on the background of the problem, this study focuses on how Islamic Religious Education (PAI) teachers at SDN 2 Kesumadadi make the students build their religious character by cultivating them through the Asma’ al-Husna (the 99 Names of Allah). Although the curriculum is only 4 names of Allah, the teachers extend the names to 99 in order to nourish the students’ souls and to reduce the fear that faced by students when dealing with Arabic. In this study, the researcher uses qualitative field research methods to gather the data by doing direct observation, deep interview, and documentation. The results show that the Islamic Religious Education teachers at the elementary school implement the collective recitation of Asma’ al-Husna within the religious education class by using a strategy that is built upon five aspects, namely uswah hasanah (good examples), habituation, mentoring (by using the Latin transliteration to replace the Arabic script), appreciating, and punishing students. The purpose of the strategy is to inculcate Islamic values to the students in order to develop their spiritual and emotional intelligence in the elementary school context.
